steps.title: Map the sentence boundary
steps.content: The first main clause is "Economists have observed that improvements in efficiency often correlate negatively with resource conservation." The next main clause is "efficiency gains, lowering the cost of use, may increase demand to the extent that resource consumption ultimately rises." The word "though" is supplementary here, so it needs comma separation from the first clause and a semicolon before the next main clause.

steps.title: Choose the punctuation that separates the parts correctly
steps.content: The referenced choice gives "conservation, though ; efficiency gains..." In the rendered sentence, the comma sets off "though" from the first main clause, and the semicolon joins the following main clause to the rest of the sentence. That is the only choice that both handles "though" and marks the boundary between the main clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the other choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — conservation, though,: leaves the next main clause joined only by commas, creating a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — conservation ; though: places the semicolon before "though," which does not correctly separate the supplementary word from the preceding clause. ✗
  • Choice C — conservation, though ;: correctly sets off "though" and uses the semicolon to join the next main clause. ✓
  • Choice D — conservation, though: lacks the semicolon needed before the following main clause, so it creates a comma splice. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
